Top Questions

### When was Thomas Edison born?

Thomas Alva Edison was born in [Milan](https://www.britannica.com/place/Milan-Ohio) , [Ohio](https://www.britannica.com/place/Ohio-state) , on February 11, 1847.

### When did Thomas Edison die?

Thomas Edison died on October 18, 1931, in [West Orange](https://www.britannica.com/place/West-Orange) , [New Jersey](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-Jersey) .

### How did Thomas Edison become famous?

Thomas Edison unveiled the [phonograph](https://www.britannica.com/technology/phonograph) —which reproduced [sounds](https://www.britannica.com/science/sound-physics) by means of the [vibration](https://www.britannica.com/science/vibration) of a stylus following a groove on a rotating disc—in December 1877. The public’s amazement surrounding this invention was quickly followed by universal acclaim. Edison was projected into worldwide prominence and was dubbed the Wizard of Menlo Park.

### How did Thomas Edison change the world?

Thomas Edison played a significant part in introducing the modern age of [electricity](https://www.britannica.com/science/electricity) . His inventions included the phonograph, the carbon-button transmitter for the [telephone](https://www.britannica.com/technology/telephone) [speaker](https://www.britannica.com/technology/loudspeaker) and [microphone](https://www.britannica.com/technology/microphone-electroacoustic-device) , the [incandescent lamp](https://www.britannica.com/technology/incandescent-lamp) , the first commercial [electric light](https://www.britannica.com/technology/lamp) and power system, an experimental electric [railroad](https://www.britannica.com/technology/railroad) , and key elements of [motion-picture](https://www.britannica.com/art/film) equipment.

Watch a silent short of Thomas Edison, who invented the phonograph and incandescent electric light Thomas Edison, seen late in life in this video, was the most famous inventor in American history. Though he is best known for his invention of the phonograph and incandescent electric light, Edison took out 1,093 patents in a variety of fields, including electric light and power, telephony and telegraphy, and sound recording. His public image as a homespun, untutored genius actually concealed a thinker who was quite systematic and methodical and who collaborated closely with machinists, designers, and scientists in his laboratory at Menlo Park, N.J. (more)

See all videos for this article

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/56/156-050-7C1C1C60/Thomas-Alva-Edison-tinfoil-phonograph-photograph-Mathew-1878.jpg)

[Thomas Edison](https://cdn.britannica.com/56/156-050-7C1C1C60/Thomas-Alva-Edison-tinfoil-phonograph-photograph-Mathew-1878.jpg) Thomas Edison demonstrates his tinfoil phonograph. Mathew Brady took this photograph in 1878. (more)

**Thomas Edison** (born February 11, 1847, [Milan](https://www.britannica.com/place/Milan-Ohio) , [Ohio](https://www.britannica.com/place/Ohio-state) , U.S.—died October 18, 1931, [West Orange](https://www.britannica.com/place/West-Orange) , New Jersey) was an American [inventor](https://www.britannica.com/topic/inventor) who, singly or jointly, held a world-record 1,093 [patents](https://www.britannica.com/topic/patent) . In addition, he created the world’s first industrial research [laboratory](https://www.britannica.com/science/laboratory-science) .

The role of chemistry in Thomas Edison's inventions How Thomas Edison changed the world. (more)

See all videos for this article

[Edison](https://www.britannica.com/place/Edison) was the quintessential American [inventor](https://www.britannica.com/topic/inventor) in the era of Yankee ingenuity. He began his career in 1863, in the adolescence of the [telegraph](https://www.britannica.com/technology/telegraph) industry, when virtually the only source of electricity was primitive [batteries](https://www.britannica.com/technology/battery-electronics) putting out a low-voltage [current](https://www.britannica.com/science/electric-current) . Before he died, in 1931, he had played a critical role in introducing the modern age of [electricity](https://www.britannica.com/science/electricity) . From his laboratories and workshops emanated the [phonograph](https://www.britannica.com/technology/phonograph) , the carbon-button transmitter for the [telephone](https://www.britannica.com/technology/telephone) speaker and [microphone](https://www.britannica.com/technology/microphone-electroacoustic-device) , the [incandescent lamp](https://www.britannica.com/technology/incandescent-lamp) , a revolutionary generator of unprecedented [efficiency](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/efficiency) , the first commercial electric light and [power](https://www.britannica.com/technology/electric-power) system, an experimental electric [railroad](https://www.britannica.com/technology/railroad) , and key elements of [motion-picture apparatus](https://www.britannica.com/technology/motion-picture-camera) , as well as a host of other inventions.

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/47/79847-050-A78604A0/Thomas-Alva-Edison.jpg)

[Thomas Edison](https://cdn.britannica.com/47/79847-050-A78604A0/Thomas-Alva-Edison.jpg) (more)

Edison was the seventh and last child—the fourth surviving—of Samuel Edison, Jr., and Nancy Elliot Edison. At an early age he developed [hearing](https://www.britannica.com/science/hearing-sense) problems, which have been variously attributed but were most likely due to a familial tendency to [mastoiditis](https://www.britannica.com/science/mastoiditis) . Whatever the cause, Edison’s [deafness](https://www.britannica.com/science/deafness) strongly influenced his behaviour and career, providing the motivation for many of his inventions.

## Early years

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/53/79853-050-9AC68819/Thomas-Alva-Edison-boy.jpg)

[Thomas Edison](https://cdn.britannica.com/53/79853-050-9AC68819/Thomas-Alva-Edison-boy.jpg) Thomas Edison as a young boy. (more)

In 1854 Samuel Edison became the [lighthouse](https://www.britannica.com/technology/lighthouse) keeper and carpenter on the Fort Gratiot military post near [Port Huron](https://www.britannica.com/place/Port-Huron) , [Michigan](https://www.britannica.com/place/Michigan) , where the family lived in a substantial home. Alva, as the inventor was known until his second marriage, entered school there and attended sporadically for five years. He was imaginative and inquisitive, but, because much instruction was by rote and he had difficulty hearing, he was bored and was labeled a misfit. To compensate, he became an [avid](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/avid) and omnivorous reader. Edison’s lack of formal schooling was not unusual. At the time of the [Civil War](https://www.britannica.com/event/American-Civil-War) the average American had attended school a total of 434 days—little more than two years’ schooling by today’s standards.

In 1859 Edison quit school and began working as a trainboy on the railroad between [Detroit](https://www.britannica.com/place/Detroit) and Port Huron. Four years earlier, the Michigan Central had initiated the commercial application of the [telegraph](https://www.britannica.com/technology/telegraph) by using it to control the movement of its trains, and the Civil War brought a vast expansion of [transportation](https://www.britannica.com/technology/transportation-technology) and [communication](https://www.britannica.com/topic/communication) . Edison took advantage of the opportunity to learn telegraphy and in 1863 became an apprentice telegrapher.

Britannica Quiz Pop Quiz: 15 Things to Know About the Industrial Revolution

Messages received on the initial [Morse](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Morse-Code) telegraph were inscribed as a series of dots and dashes on a strip of [paper](https://www.britannica.com/technology/paper) that was decoded and read, so Edison’s partial deafness was no handicap. Receivers were increasingly being equipped with a sounding key, however, enabling telegraphers to “read” messages by the clicks. The transformation of telegraphy to an auditory art left Edison more and more disadvantaged during his six-year career as an itinerant telegrapher in the Midwest, the South, [Canada](https://www.britannica.com/place/Canada) , and [New England](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-England) . Amply supplied with ingenuity and insight, he devoted much of his energy toward improving the inchoate equipment and inventing devices to [facilitate](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/facilitate) some of the tasks that his physical limitations made difficult. By January 1869 he had made enough progress with a duplex [telegraph](https://www.britannica.com/technology/telegraph) (a device capable of transmitting two messages simultaneously on one wire) and a [printer](https://www.britannica.com/technology/computer-printer) , which converted electrical signals to letters, that he abandoned telegraphy for full-time [invention](https://www.britannica.com/technology/invention-technology) and entrepreneurship.

See all related content

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/55/79855-050-E3556E91/Thomas-Alva-Edison-man.jpg)

[Thomas Edison](https://cdn.britannica.com/55/79855-050-E3556E91/Thomas-Alva-Edison-man.jpg) Thomas Edison as a young man. (more)

Edison moved to [New York City](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-York-City) , where he initially went into partnership with Frank L. Pope, a noted electrical expert, to produce the Edison Universal Stock Printer and other printing telegraphs. Between 1870 and 1875 he worked out of [Newark](https://www.britannica.com/place/Newark-New-Jersey) , [New Jersey](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-Jersey) , and was involved in a variety of partnerships and complex transactions in the fiercely competitive and [convoluted](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/convoluted) telegraph industry, which was dominated by the [Western Union Telegraph Company](https://www.britannica.com/money/Western-Union-Corporation) . As an independent [entrepreneur](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/entrepreneur) he was available to the highest bidder and played both sides against the middle. During this period he worked on improving an automatic telegraph system for Western Union’s rivals. The automatic telegraph, which recorded messages by means of a [chemical reaction](https://www.britannica.com/science/chemical-reaction) engendered by the electrical transmissions, proved of limited commercial success, but the work advanced Edison’s knowledge of [chemistry](https://www.britannica.com/science/chemistry) and laid the basis for his development of the electric pen and [mimeograph](https://www.britannica.com/technology/mimeograph) , both important devices in the early office [machine](https://www.britannica.com/technology/machine) industry, and indirectly led to the discovery of the [phonograph](https://www.britannica.com/technology/phonograph) . Under the [aegis](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/aegis) of Western Union he devised the quadruplex, capable of transmitting four messages simultaneously over one wire, but railroad baron and [Wall Street](https://www.britannica.com/money/Wall-Street-New-York-City) financier [Jay Gould](https://www.britannica.com/money/Jay-Gould) , Western Union’s bitter rival, snatched the quadruplex from the telegraph company’s grasp in December 1874 by paying Edison more than $100,000 in cash, bonds, and stock, one of the larger payments for any invention up to that time. Years of litigation followed.
